notes.text: A worker is killed during a velociraptor transfer at a remote island facility. In the same sequence, we learn that the park's investors have demanded an independent safety review before they will continue funding — the inciting pressure that sets everything in motion.

A Montana badlands dig site: Grant and Sattler excavate in heat and dust, surrounded by the bones of creatures that have been safely dead for 65 million years. It is the world Grant prefers — ordered, knowable, past.

Alan Grant is a man in love with extinction. He studies dinosaurs precisely because they are gone — controllable as fossils, dangerous only as ideas. His pointed dismissal of a boy who compares a raptor to a turkey is the film's first laugh and its first warning.

John Hammond arrives at the dig site by helicopter — wealthy, paternal, and utterly certain that what he has built on Isla Nublar is a gift to the world. His blindness to risk is established immediately: he offers Grant and Sattler funding they cannot refuse.

The setup poses the film's central question in the gap between Grant's world and Hammond's: should we resurrect what extinction has decided? The badlands are full of answers Hammond has not thought to ask.

The helicopter descends through clouds to reveal Isla Nublar — and a Brachiosaurus grazes beside the road. Grant stumbles out of the jeep in disbelief. The past is alive. Every assumption he has built his career on has been overturned in thirty seconds.

Ian Malcolm joins the review group — mathematician, chaos theorist, and the film's most articulate prophet of disaster. He is not a villain but a corrective: his objections are precise, principled, and ignored.

Hammond's grandchildren Tim and Lex arrive at the park — an unwanted complication for Grant, who makes no secret of his discomfort with children. They will become the mirror in which his transformation is reflected.

The inciting incident is not the dinosaur sighting — it is the revelation that control was always the illusion. Hammond has cloned life and caged it, but the velociraptor pen at the opening told us how that story ends.

The tour begins and immediately stalls — the T-Rex doesn't appear, the sick triceratops reveals an animal welfare crisis, and Dennis Nedry executes his plan to steal embryos and disable the park's systems for eighteen minutes. A tropical storm is closing in.

Nedry is the human face of the park's failure — not a monster but a disgruntled contractor who was underpaid, undervalued, and underestimated. Hammond's arrogance produced him as surely as it produced the dinosaurs.

Stranded in the tour vehicles during the power outage, Grant is forced into proximity with Tim and Lex. He has no choice but to engage — and discovers, reluctantly, that children are not so different from the creatures he has spent his life studying.
Malcolm's chaos theory argument — that complex systems resist control, that life is inherently unpredictable — is not philosophy but prophecy. Every complication in Act II is his thesis made flesh.

The progressive complications reveal that the park was never in control — Nedry's betrayal just made visible what was already true. The system's brittleness was built in from the first strand of cloned DNA.

The T-Rex breaks through its paddock fence and attacks the tour vehicles. Grant and Malcolm protect the children; Malcolm is injured; Grant leads Tim and Lex into the jungle. The park has become a wilderness and there is no going back.
The T-Rex attack is Grant's midpoint: he stops being a spectator of the crisis and becomes responsible for two children's lives. The man who feared the future is now its only protection.

Hammond, watching from the control room as the disaster unfolds, begins his slow reckoning. 'When you get on the other side of the glass, the problem isn't so goddamn funny, is it?' Malcolm says — and Hammond has no answer.

The midpoint crystallises the film's argument: the moment the fences come down, all Hammond's rationalisation collapses. Nature is not a product. The T-Rex did not get the memo about staying in its paddock.

Grant, Tim, and Lex navigate the island on foot — electrified fences, a hatching raptor nest, a Brachiosaurus encounter. Meanwhile, the velociraptors escape and kill park warden Muldoon. The hunters are loose and smarter than anyone prepared for.

The raptors are the crisis's true antagonists — not dumb predators but coordinated hunters who test fences for weaknesses and set traps. They make Hammond's promise of control look not merely optimistic but delusional.

Hammond sits with Malcolm in the medical bay and says, 'I don't understand why things didn't work.' Malcolm's reply — 'Because your scientists were so preoccupied with whether they could, they never stopped to think if they should' — is the film's thematic peak.

'Life finds a way.' Malcolm's line, first spoken as theory, is now biological fact: the dinosaurs have been breeding. The all-female population was not a control — it was a delay. The crisis proves that life is not a system to be engineered but a force to be reckoned with.

Grant and the children reach the Visitor Centre. Lex, a self-described 'hacker,' restores the park's computer systems — locking the doors, rebooting security, and allowing the survivors to call for rescue. The path to the ending opens.

Lex's competence is the break into Act III. The children Grant has been protecting rescue him — and everyone else. His arc turns on their capability, not just their vulnerability: he has been changed by having to trust them.

The solution comes not from adult expertise or Hammond's technology but from a child who grew up with computers. The film quietly argues that the future belongs to those willing to engage with it — not those who would rather stay in the past.

Two raptors hunt Grant and the children through the control room. Just as they are cornered, the T-Rex crashes through the wall and kills the raptors — not to save the humans, but because it is hungry. The survivors run for the helicopter.

The T-Rex's intervention is the film's famous flourish: the same chaos that threatened the survivors also saves them. Nature is indifferent, not malevolent — and that indifference is, in this moment, a kind of grace.

The climax resolves not through human ingenuity defeating nature, but through nature's own chaos. Grant did not outthink the raptors — he outlasted them long enough for something larger to arrive. Survival is earned by endurance, not mastery.

The helicopter lifts off over the ocean. Grant holds Tim and Lex as they sleep against him. Hammond, across the cabin, meets Grant's gaze — and says nothing. Outside, pelicans fly in formation over the water.

Grant holds the children without hesitation or discomfort — the man who flinched from a boy's touch in the first scene. The transformation is visible in a single image: he has become someone capable of caring for the future.
Hammond stares out the window, clutching his amber-headed cane. He is not broken, exactly — but the certainty is gone. He built a monument to the past and learned that the past cannot be owned.

The resolution does not punish Hammond or celebrate Grant's survival. It simply shows two men who have been changed by what they witnessed — one toward humility, one toward openness. The pelicans fly on, magnificently indifferent.

The final image mirrors the film's opening: not a direct echo, but a rhyme. Where the opening showed nature as pure danger, the closing shows it as simply itself — free, ancient, and entirely unconcerned with human plans for it.
